medical_history.history_of_present_illness: First episode of rhabdomyolysis at age 9 years. Many episodes of myalgia associated with 'red urine' until age 22 years. Severe episode of rhabdomyolysis produced acute renal failure (creatine kinase, CK=540,000 U/l; myoglobin=11,500 ug/l). Secondary carnitine deficiency. No hypoglycemia or cardiac involvement.

laboratory_examinations.findings: Increased concentrations of long chain acylcarnitines (C16:0, C14:0, and C14:1). The C14:1 species is the dominant acylcarnitine observed in blood from these patients.

VLCAD deficiency was confirmed in all 16 cell lines utilized in these experiments by direct enzyme assay. Ten of these cell lines were derived from patients with the cardiomyopathic form of the disease. Of the remaining, four had only recurrent hypoglycemia and two had late onset myopathy.
Hypoglycemia observed in patients with VLCAD deficiency.
Increased serum CK activities observed in patients with VLCAD deficiency.
Increased serum aminotransferases observed in patients with VLCAD deficiency.

radiographic_examinations.findings: Hypertrophic or dilated cardiomyopathy observed in patients with VLCAD deficiency.
radiographic_examinations.changes_over_time: Cardiac hypertrophy had disappeared after treatment in some patients.

other_tests.findings: Analysis of acylcarnitine intermediates following incubation of intact human cells with fatty acids revealed that the milder clinical phenotypes could be distinguished from the severe cardiomyopathic phenotype.
Used for quantitation of individual acylcarnitines.
